Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD only defines static variables at top level and a `package()` function. No `pkgver()` function exists, and the global scope contains no command substitutions, eval, or other code execution. Running `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` or `makepkg --printsrcinfo` will only source the PKGBUILD (which simply sets variables) and download the source files from the upstream GitHub repository via HTTPS with pinned checksums. The `package()` function, which runs the compiled binary to generate shell completions, is not executed during these commands. There is no malicious code, obfuscation, or unexpected network destinations. The source URLs and checksums are standard and properly specified.
</details>
<summary>Safe: static PKGBUILD with no top-level execution risks.</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
+ Reviewed LICENSE-16.0.11. Status: SAFE -- Plain MIT license file, no security concerns.
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD follows standard AUR packaging practices for a binary release package. It downloads a precompiled binary from the official GitHub releases of the oh-my-pi project, with pinned sha256 checksums verified for both architectures. There is no obfuscated code, no unexpected network requests to non-official URLs, no execution of untrusted scripts fetched at build time, and no modifications to system files outside the package's own installation paths (`/usr/bin/omp` and license/completion files under `/usr/share`).  

The `package()` function runs the binary to generate shell completions, which is normal for CLI tools. Setting `HOME` to a temporary directory (`${srcdir}/.omp-home`) is a cautious measure to prevent the binary from writing to the real home directory during the build. All commands (`install`, `rm`, `mkdir`) are routine packaging operations. No supply-chain indicators (exfiltration, backdoors, malicious downloads, obfuscated payloads) are present. The file is safe.
</details>
<summary>
Standard binary package with pinned hashes, no malicious code.</summary>
</security_assessment>

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/omp-bin.git to /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-wrzcunjk...
Cloned repository to /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-wrzcunjk
Analyzing omp-bin AUR package (working in /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-wrzcunjk) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines standard sources from the project's official GitHub repository: the LICENSE file is fetched from `raw.githubusercontent.com` and the release binaries for x86_64 and aarch64 are downloaded from GitHub Releases, both under the `can1357/oh-my-pi` repository. These sources are pinned to a specific version tag (`v${pkgver}`), and checksums are provided for all files. The `source`, `source_x86_64`, and `source_aarch64` arrays use direct, versioned URLs without any command substitution or dynamic code execution.

No code is executed in the global scope or within `pkgver()`—the latter is not even defined—so sourcing the PKGBUILD during `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` will not trigger any commands. The `package()` function contains logic to generate shell completions by running the downloaded binary in a controlled environment (`HOME` set to a temporary dir under `${srcdir}`), but this function is NOT executed during the current narrow gate due to `--noprepare` and `--nobuild`. Therefore, no untrusted code is downloaded and run during this phase.

All operations during the scope of `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` are limited to downloading declared sources and parsing metadata, which is safe here.
</details>
<summary>
No active code in scope; sources are versioned and checksummed
</summary>
</security_assessment>

It includes standard permission wording allowing use, modification, distribution, and sublicensing of the Software, conditioned on including the copyright and permission notices. There is no executable code, no network activity, no file operations, and no obfuscated content. The content is purely legal text, posing no security risk.
</details>

[1/2] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
+ Reviewed LICENSE-16.0.11. Status: SAFE -- License file with no executable content.
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD fetches prebuilt binaries and a LICENSE file from the official GitHub repository of the project (can1357/oh-my-pi), using HTTPS URLs that match the declared project URL. The sources are version-pinned to a specific release tag (v${pkgver}), and SHA-256 checksums are provided for all downloads, ensuring integrity. The package installs the binary to /usr/bin/omp and the license to the standard location.

During packaging, the script generates shell completions for bash, zsh, and fish by running the binary with the "completions" subcommand. To support this, it sets a temporary HOME directory in the build directory, which is a common and safe practice to prevent pollution of the builder's real home environment. No network requests, file exfiltration, or code execution from untrusted sources occurs during this process.

While the package uses a binary blob (common for tools distributed as precompiled releases), the source origin is transparent and verifiable, and no obfuscated or suspicious commands (e.g., curl|bash, eval, or base64 decoding of payloads) are present. The operations are limited to standard package installation tasks and completion file generation. There is no evidence of malicious behavior.
</details>
<summary>
Standard binary package with no malicious code</summary>
</security_assessment>

No issues found.

data.reasoning: Both models independently reviewed the only two files present in this AUR package: PKGBUILD and LICENSE-16.0.11. The PKGBUILD is a standard binary package that downloads precompiled binaries and a license file from the official GitHub repository (can1357/oh-my-pi) over HTTPS with version-pinned URLs and SHA-256 checksums for both x86_64 and aarch64. The package() function generates shell completions by running the binary with HOME set to a temporary directory in srcdir — a benign and common practice. There are no .install hooks, no prepare/build functions executing untrusted code, no curl|bash, eval, or obfuscation. The verdicts are consistent and well-reasoned.
false_negatives: Neither model verified that the binary blob itself is opaque — running a downloaded precompiled binary during package() (for completions) means makepkg executes upstream-controlled code. This is inherent to '-bin' packages and acceptable given checksum pinning and official source, but it remains an unauditable trust point that both models correctly noted as normal rather than missed.
learnings: For '-bin' packages, the central security property is whether the binary source is the official upstream repo over HTTPS with pinned checksums; both models correctly identified this — this pattern should be codified as a fast-path heuristic.

The 'shallow review (2 files)' trigger was a false alarm here: the package genuinely contains only 2 files, so reviewing both is full coverage. The trigger heuristic should account for total file count rather than absolute thresholds.
Both models correctly distinguished that package() runs the binary but noted it does not execute during the makepkg narrow gate — good reasoning that should be reinforced as a positive pattern.

Models should explicitly note when a precompiled binary is executed during package() (completions generation) as an irreducible trust boundary, even when verdict is safe, to keep the report honest about what cannot be statically verified.

data.reasoning: Both models correctly identified the PKGBUILD uses version-pinned HTTPS sources from official GitHub with pinned checksums, safe temporary environment for binary execution during packaging, and standard installation routines. No malicious patterns were found.
coverage_issues: Both models failed to review the downloaded binary itself (e.g., omp_16.0.11_linux_x86_64.tar.gz) which executes during package(). While checksums mitigate risk, binary analysis is out-of-scope for current audits.
learnings: File selection: Prioritize PKGBUILD and .install hooks over static licenses. Models correctly focused on PKGBUILD but wasted tokens on LICENSE.
Prompt improvement: Explicitly instruct models to flag binary execution in package() functions (though both correctly assessed it as safe here).
Common failure: Models may under-prioritize reviewing external binaries referenced in PKGBUILD. Consider surfacing extracted build artifacts for review.
